---
title: "II — Devices and analog circuits"
description: "From semiconductor operating regions to bounded, measured signal chains."
page-role: part
part-id: analog
---

Part I made a circuit observable and its measurements defensible. Part II asks
what happens when the circuit elements are nonlinear, active, sensitive to
temperature and frequency, and able to convert one physical quantity into
another. The goal is not a catalogue of ideal diode, transistor, and amplifier
circuits. It is the ability to choose an operating region, predict which
non-ideal effect matters, and test whether a real device or signal chain still
meets its requirement.

The same circuit remains one object described in several languages: carrier
behavior in a material, a schematic symbol and terminal references, a current–
voltage curve, a large- or small-signal equation, a simulation, a datasheet
limit, a physical layout, and a measured waveform. Agreement among those
descriptions is understanding; disagreement reveals a wrong operating region,
an omitted parasitic, an intrusive measurement, or a claim stronger than the
evidence. This part develops that agreement from the semiconductor junction to
the calibrated analog signal chain [@sedra2020microelectronic].

## The part question

> How can physical devices shape, switch, amplify, sense, and present real
> signals while remaining within finite voltage, current, power, temperature,
> noise, bandwidth, linearity, and stability limits?

“Shape” includes rectification, limiting, switching, gain, filtering, and
conversion between physical and electrical quantities. “Real signals” means
that amplitude type, source and load impedance, bandwidth, reference, operating
conditions, and uncertainty are stated. “Remaining within limits” means more
than obtaining the desired nominal output: ratings, tolerances, temperature,
parasitics, and measurement loading must leave defensible margin.

## Why devices and analog circuits come second

Part I supplied the conservation laws and passive-circuit methods. Active
devices do not replace them. A diode operating point is still the intersection
of a device relation and a source–load network; transistor dissipation still
belongs in an energy and thermal balance; amplifier gain is meaningful only
with defined source, load, frequency, and amplitude conditions; and a sensor
output becomes evidence only through the measurement discipline of F06.

This part adds four capabilities that the later book assumes:

- **operating-region reasoning**, so a device is treated differently in cutoff,
  forward conduction, active operation, saturation, breakdown, or recovery;
- **incremental reasoning**, so a nonlinear device can be analyzed around a
  declared bias point without mistaking a local relation for a global one;
- **feedback and error budgeting**, so gain, sensitivity, noise, distortion,
  bandwidth, and stability become allocated quantities rather than surprises;
  and
- **transduction and presentation**, so a physical measurand can become a
  calibrated electrical signal and a system state can be shown to a person
  without ambiguity.

These are the bridge between the electrical foundations and digital logic,
sampling, control, power conversion, PCB realization, and complete instruments.

## Entry knowledge

The formal prerequisites are recorded in
[`chapters.toml`](../chapters.toml), but the whole part assumes the working habits
of Part I. In particular, you should be able to:

- reduce a source–load network and find a DC operating point using F03–F04;
- account for power, stored energy, temperature rise, and component ratings
  using F05 and F07;
- plan a measurement, include instrument loading, state uncertainty, and debug
  by hypothesis using F06; and
- use impedance, phasors, transfer functions, and frequency response from F08.

Use [M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) just in time for
logarithms, decibels, complex impedance, and transfer functions. Keep
[T01](../appendices/t01-datasheets-standards.qmd) beside the device-selection
work: a typical curve, an absolute maximum rating, a guaranteed limit, and a
test condition answer different questions. [M03](../appendices/m03-probability-numerical.qmd)
provides optional support for deeper calibration, uncertainty propagation, and
statistical-variation work in A05–A07; it is not a formal Part II prerequisite
in the registry.

Practical work remains current-limited and extra-low-voltage unless an approved
laboratory procedure explicitly establishes another boundary. A component
described as an isolator, a protected switch, or a light source does not by
itself authorize connection to hazardous energy or direct viewing of an
unqualified optical source.

## The learning arc

The eight chapters move from *device behavior* to *controlled signal chains*,
then branch into integrated implementation and human-facing optoelectronics.
Page order is a coherent teaching route; the exact prerequisite graph appears
below.

| Chapter | Central question it answers | You leave able to | Hands forward |
|---|---|---|---|
| [A01](a01-semiconductor-diodes.qmd) | How does material physics produce direction- and voltage-dependent conduction? | relate junction behavior to diode curves; choose bounded approximations; design rectifying, clamping, limiting, protection, and basic light-sensitive or light-emitting circuits | junction behavior, temperature dependence, recovery, and ratings for A02 and A08 |
| [A02](a02-transistors.qmd) | How can a small electrical signal control a larger current or voltage safely? | place a BJT or MOSFET in a declared region; design low-side, high-side, complementary, and inductive-load switching stages; check drive, loss, safe operating area, and thermal margin | controlled-source and switching behavior for A03, A07, D01, and S05 |
| [A03](a03-bias-small-signal-amplifiers.qmd) | How can a nonlinear transistor amplify a small changing signal predictably? | establish a bias point, derive an incremental description, and predict gain, loading, headroom, bandwidth, and the onset of distortion in common-emitter or common-source stages | transistor-level gain and loading for A04 and A07 |
| [A04](a04-op-amps-feedback.qmd) | When does feedback make amplifier behavior predictable, and when does the ideal op-amp approximation fail? | analyze inverting, non-inverting, summing, difference, filtering, integrating, and comparison functions; check range, offset, bias, slew, bandwidth, saturation, recovery, and stability | feedback-based conditioning for A05, A06, R02, and S04 |
| [A05](a05-sensors-instrumentation.qmd) | How does a physical quantity become a calibrated electrical measurement? | construct a measurand-to-decision chain using sensors, excitation, bridges, instrumentation gain, calibration, linearization, protection, grounding, shielding, isolation, and an uncertainty budget | trustworthy analog inputs for A08, S02, R05, and X01 |
| [A06](a06-noise-nonlinearity-stability.qmd) | Which non-ideal effect limits the smallest trustworthy signal in a real analog chain? | calculate input- and output-referred error terms; relate noise density to bandwidth; assess dynamic range, clipping, distortion, intermodulation, and stability; choose mitigation with explicit trade-offs | bounded signal-chain performance for S02, R03, R05, and X01 |
| [A07](a07-integrated-circuits.qmd) | What changes when transistors and interconnect are designed together on silicon? | reason from CMOS structures to inverters, mirrors, differential pairs, bias and gain stages; assess matching, corners, parasitics, layout-dependent effects, hierarchy, packaging, and test | physical integrated implementation for advanced digital, mixed-signal, and realization work |
| [A08](a08-optoelectronics-hmi.qmd) | How can a system convert between electricity and light and present state without misleading a person? | design LED and photodetector interfaces; assess optical isolation; select and drive displays; specify contrast, ambient-light behavior, power, legibility, and accessible indicator semantics | optical sensing and usable presentation for M2, embedded interfaces, and field systems |

Grouped by the development they carry, the arc has four movements:

- **A01–A02 — establish device behavior and switching limits.** Energy bands,
  carriers, doping, junctions, and current–voltage behavior lead to diode and
  transistor operating regions. Rectifiers, clamps, protection, logic drive,
  complementary stages, and inductive loads make the regions useful. Leakage,
  temperature, reverse recovery, drive limits, safe operating area, switching
  loss, and heat show where the first approximation fails.
- **A03–A04 — turn nonlinear gain into controlled gain.** Bias fixes the point
  around which an incremental description is valid. Source and load resistance,
  coupling, bypassing, headroom, bandwidth, and distortion bound a transistor
  stage. Operational amplifiers and negative feedback then make useful transfer
  relations less dependent on device parameters, but only while open-loop gain,
  input and output ranges, slew rate, bandwidth, saturation recovery, and loop
  stability permit it.
- **A05–A06 — make an analog result trustworthy.** Sensors, excitation, bridges,
  instrumentation amplifiers, grounding, shielding, isolation, calibration, and
  traceability form a complete path from measurand to decision. Thermal, shot,
  and flicker noise; interference; nonlinearity; clipping; intermodulation;
  finite bandwidth; and unintended feedback are referred to a common point and
  combined only under stated assumptions. The result is an error budget and an
  acceptance claim, not merely a plausible waveform [@jcgm2008gum].
- **A07–A08 — connect circuits to physical implementation and people.** A07
  moves inside the integrated circuit, where geometry, process variation,
  matching, parasitics, design rules, corners, and layout verification shape
  behavior. A08 moves across an optical and human boundary, where radiant and
  luminous quantities, ambient light, isolation, display drive, readability,
  accessibility, and interpretation determine whether electrical state was
  conveyed successfully.

## Scope boundaries and intentional overlaps

Several subjects appear in more than one chapter because the representation or
decision changes. Their ownership is explicit:

- A01 introduces LED and photodiode behavior as consequences of semiconductor
  junction physics; A08 owns complete optical emitters, detectors, isolation,
  displays, and human-facing indication.
- A03 introduces distortion, bandwidth, and noise where they first limit one
  transistor amplifier; A04 introduces feedback stability and op-amp non-ideal
  behavior; A06 owns chain-level noise, nonlinearity, bandwidth, dynamic range,
  and stability budgets.
- A07 treats a CMOS inverter as a transistor-level integrated block whose
  transfer, delay, leakage, matching, and layout matter. D01 begins where ranges
  of electrical states are abstracted into logic values, and R02 owns board-level
  schematic capture and PCB layout.
- A05 ends with a conditioned, calibrated analog quantity. Sampling,
  quantization, and converter architectures belong to S02; production metrology
  and test decisions belong to R05.
- A02 develops device switching at current-limited bench scale. Converter
  topologies, magnetics, switching control, and power-stage design belong to S05,
  where their additional energy hazards are treated explicitly.

The part therefore covers the continuous path from semiconductor behavior to a
bounded analog interface. It does not attempt to absorb digital design, data
conversion, control theory, power electronics, PCB realization, or product
qualification merely because each depends on analog behavior.

## How the chapters connect

The dependency structure has a common device trunk and several purposeful
branches. A05 and A06 are parallel developments after A04; A07 can be studied as
soon as A02–A03 are held; A08 joins junction physics from A01 to the measurement
chain from A05.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-analog-dependencies
%%| fig-cap: "The direct prerequisite graph for Part II. Every arrow means that the source chapter is a prerequisite of the destination in `chapters.toml`; page order and broader conceptual handoffs are described separately."
%%| fig-alt: "A vertical prerequisite graph. F04 and F05 lead to A01. A01 and F05 lead to A02. A02 and F08 lead to A03. A03 and F08 lead to A04. A04 and F06 lead to A05. A04 and F08 lead to A06. A02 and A03 lead to A07. A01 and A05 lead to A08."
%%| fig-width: 6.1
flowchart TB
  F04["Part I · F04<br/>network equivalents"]
  F05["Part I · F05<br/>power and heat"]
  F06["Part I · F06<br/>measurement"]
  F08["Part I · F08<br/>frequency response"]

  A01["A01 · Diodes"]
  A02["A02 · Transistors"]
  A03["A03 · Bias and gain"]
  A04["A04 · Op-amps and feedback"]
  A05["A05 · Instrumentation"]
  A06["A06 · Analog limits"]
  A07["A07 · Integrated circuits"]
  A08["A08 · Optoelectronics and HMI"]

  F04 --> A01
  F05 --> A01
  F05 --> A02
  A01 --> A02
  F08 --> A03
  A02 --> A03
  F08 --> A04
  A03 --> A04
  F06 --> A05
  A04 --> A05
  F08 --> A06
  A04 --> A06
  A02 --> A07
  A03 --> A07
  A01 --> A08
  A05 --> A08
```

The arrows in @fig-analog-dependencies are deliberately narrower in meaning than
the routes below. They encode direct prerequisites, not every useful conceptual
handoff and not the order in which the pages happen to appear. Four routes make
the part easier to navigate:

- **The active-device trunk — A01 → A02 → A03 → A04.** Terminal behavior leads
  to switching, incremental gain, and feedback-controlled gain. F05 and F08 join
  where thermal and frequency-dependent reasoning first become necessary.
- **The precision-signal route — A03–A06.** Bias and feedback support two
  parallel capabilities: A05 constructs the calibrated measurement chain, while
  A06 establishes its noise, nonlinearity, bandwidth, and stability limits.
  These capabilities meet again in [S02](../05-domains/s02-sampling-data-conversion.qmd),
  [R05](../06-realization/r05-metrology-reliability-test.qmd), and
  [X01](../07-systems/x01-precision-instrument.qmd), although those downstream
  chapters retain their own exact prerequisites.
- **The integrated branch — A02 and A03 → A07.** It can be taken before A04–A06
  when the destination is device geometry, matching, process variation, and
  layout-dependent circuit behavior.
- **The optical/HMI merge — A01 and A05 → A08.** It combines junction-level
  emission and detection with the conditioned measurement path. A01 does not
  depend on A05, and A05 does not depend on A01; both are required only when the
  branches meet at A08.

The integrated and optical/HMI branches prevent “analog” from meaning only
board-level op-amp circuits: device geometry and human interpretation are both
physical parts of the system.

## How to reason through an analog design

The book-wide cycle remains **predict, build, measure, reconcile, decide**, but
analog work gives each step a sharper form [@horowitz2015art]:

1. **Declare the signal and boundary.** Name terminals, reference polarities and
   current directions, source and load, amplitude type, frequency range,
   temperature, supply range, and the quantity that must be decided.
2. **Choose the operating region.** Mark the expected DC point and signal
   excursion on a device curve or transfer relation. State whether the relation
   is exact, empirical, piecewise, or a local approximation.
3. **Predict at more than one scale.** Estimate first; calculate the DC point,
   incremental behavior, power, and dominant error; then check a limiting case
   and the first second-order effect likely to break the result.
4. **Build or simulate the same named circuit.** Keep device identity,
   references, source and load impedances, parasitics, test points, and operating
   conditions synchronized. Simulation is calculated evidence, not measurement.
5. **Measure without hiding intrusion.** Record configuration, instruments,
   loading, bandwidth, raw observations, and uncertainty. Sweep the condition
   most likely to expose cutoff, saturation, clipping, instability, thermal
   drift, or noise.
6. **Reconcile and decide.** Assign every discrepancy only as far as independent
   evidence permits, then apply a requirement with margin and a stated decision
   rule. A nominal curve or typical datasheet value is not a worst-case pass.

This process keeps the physical device, schematic, equations, simulation,
datasheet, layout, and bench result attached to the same claim.

## Turn reading into evidence

The principal practical sequence is L06–L08. All three are catalogued as
current-limited, extra-low-voltage work (safety level **L1**), but each still
requires defined apparatus, stop conditions, and local authorization before it
becomes a deliverable.

| Evidence goal | Aligned chapters | Level | What it demonstrates |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L06** Diode and transistor operating regions | A01, A02 | L1 | measured terminal curves and switching waveforms identify the intended region and expose temperature, drive, loading, or recovery limits |
| **L07** Amplifier gain, limits, and feedback | A03, A04, A06 | L1 | DC bias, small-signal gain, frequency response, clipping, noise, and stability evidence agree with a bounded amplifier prediction |
| **L08** Sensor calibration and uncertainty | A05, F06, R05 | L1 | a defined measurand passes through excitation, conditioning, calibration, and uncertainty analysis to an explicit decision |

Two milestones integrate the part rather than treating it as an isolated course:

- **M2 — build a calibrated environmental monitor** combines F07–F08,
  A01–A02, A08, E01, and R01. It uses device protection, switching, optical or
  visual presentation, calibration, and use-context requirements to produce a
  two-variable monitor with a defensible claim.
- **M3 — create a precision instrument** combines A03–A06 with S01–S02, R05,
  and X01. It turns gain, feedback, sensing, noise, bandwidth, calibration, and
  conversion into a measured signal chain with an uncertainty statement.

A07's principal evidence is different: transistor-level simulation across
process, voltage, and temperature, followed by a layout-aware parasitic
comparison and a bounded block-level claim. That is valid design evidence, but
it is not relabeled as a fabricated measurement. The full integration map is on
the [practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine).

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary"}
The aligned analog labs are deliberately current-limited and extra-low-voltage.
Reading these chapters is not authorization for mains-connected circuits,
high-energy batteries, high-current inductive loads, unqualified optical
sources, medical interfaces, or safety isolation. An optocoupler, protection
diode, current limit, or simulator does not establish a safe system by itself.
Use approved facilities, supervision, components, procedures, and stop
conditions, and stop when the safety boundary or evidence is inadequate.
:::

## Exit capability

After this part you should be able to translate a physical behavior or sensing
requirement into a current-limited analog circuit, choose and justify its device
operating regions, predict its DC and frequency-dependent behavior, measure the
quantities that could falsify the prediction, and make a bounded decision from
an error budget. Expressed as a checklist, you can:

- read a device curve and datasheet without confusing typical behavior,
  guaranteed limits, test conditions, and absolute maximum ratings;
- select diode, BJT, MOSFET, op-amp, sensor, and optoelectronic approximations
  whose operating conditions and failure boundaries are explicit;
- establish bias and headroom, derive local gain and impedances, and identify
  cutoff, saturation, clipping, slew limitation, or thermal failure;
- use negative feedback while checking input/output range, finite gain and
  bandwidth, stability, saturation, and recovery;
- construct a measurand-to-decision chain with excitation, protection,
  calibration, linearization, grounding or isolation, and uncertainty;
- refer noise, distortion, interference, and bandwidth limits to a common point
  and identify the dominant term without double-counting it;
- explain how process, geometry, matching, parasitics, layout, package, and test
  alter an integrated block; and
- design an optical interface or indicator whose electrical margin, ambient
  conditions, power, readability, and accessible meaning are specified.

The exit claim is intentionally bounded. Completing outlines or calculations
alone does not qualify hardware. Physical performance claims require the
appropriate rung of evidence, and hazardous or regulated applications require
competence, facilities, standards, and authorization beyond this part.

## What this hands to the rest of the book

Part II makes signals controllable before later parts make them computable,
sampled, powered, laid out, or qualified. D01 receives a transistor switch and
electrical noise margin before introducing the bit [@harris2021digital]. S02
receives a conditioned sensor signal and an error budget before sampling and
quantization. S04 receives practical gain and feedback limits; S05 receives
device switching, drive, loss, and thermal reasoning before adding power-stage
energy and control. R02–R03 receive circuits whose source/load impedances,
bandwidth, stability, grounding, matching, and parasitic sensitivities are
already named. R05 and X01 receive the calibrated precision chain whose final
claim must survive metrology and system-level verification.

The durable handoff is not a list of canonical circuits. It is the ability to
say which physical effect establishes the operating point, which approximation
predicts the desired behavior, which non-ideal term sets the limit, which test
would expose failure, and how much the available evidence permits you to claim.

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity"}
This is a development edition. The scope, page order, prerequisite structure,
chapter handoffs, and practical alignment of Part II are complete. A01–A08 are
currently outlines: their central questions and concept boundaries are stable,
but full exposition, worked results, exercises, citations, and practical
evidence still require authoring and review. An outline is a scope commitment,
not yet a teachable chapter or safety authorization. The
[reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd#interpret-chapter-status) explains what each
maturity level lets a reader rely on.
:::
